Korean language

# Korean language

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Mar 10, 2021

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[Korean](https://www.quora.com/Why-is-the-Korean-language-unique-and-how-did-it-develop-They-say-it-is-the-only-one-in-its-branch-but-what-does-this-mean/answer/Ratnakar-Sadasyula-%E0%B0%B0%E0%B0%A4%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%A8%E0%B0%BE%E0%B0%95%E0%B0%B0%E0%B1%8D-%E0%B0%B8%E0%B0%A6%E0%B0%B8%E0%B1%8D%E0%B0%AF%E0%B1%81%E0%B0%B2?__filter__=5&__nsrc__=1&__sncid__=12986298705&__snid3__=18476869036) is classified as a language isolate, in that it is not related to any specific language family or other languages. However Korean does have some influences of Chinese, which was introduced during the 3 Kingdom period. The Korean script Hanja, was adapted mostly from Chinese characters. However Hanja remained more or less an elite language, with the common people not able to read or write in it. King Sejong the Great, feeling the use of Hanja was restricted, devised the Hangul, for the masses. This Hangul is now the standard alphabet and script used in Korea, as well as certain Korean autonomous areas in China. The difference as you can see is while Hanja is more of Chinese, Hangul has it’s own style making it easier to adopt.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!c7TH!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fbucketeer-e05bbc84-baa3-437e-9518-adb32be77984.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F526cf81f-59d2-40df-95cc-3bb9609735b2_469x469.webp)

Hanja

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Hangul

Unlike the standard sequential style of the Western languages, Hangul letters are put in a block, which constitutes a syllable. So something like Han would be 한 which is basically 3 letters grouped into one- ㅎ*h*, ㅏ*a*, and ㄴ*n.*Each syllable block consists of 2 to 6 letters, which are then arranged either left to right or top to bottom.

What we know as the Hangul, was developed by the Hall of Worthies, a group of scholars, under King Sejong. Completed some time in late 1446, the entire research done by them was documented in Hunmin Jeongeum, that roughly translates to “The Proper Sounds for the Education of the People” which also accounts for the name. The advantage with Hangul, was that it was far more simpler, compared to the complex Chinese characters, and began to be widely adopted by common people.

Hangul however faced opposition from the literary elite in Korea, especially the Confucian scholars. It could be due to the fact that they saw, Hangul as a threat to their dominance over the narrative. However they could not really stop the rapid adoption of Hangul by the ordinary masses, as it spread widely. So powerful was it’s reach, that paranoid rulers like Yeonsangun and King Jungjong, banned it’s use and propagation.

However the late 16th and 17th centuries saw a revival of Hangul, especially with the gasa literature, a form of poetry. Novels in Hangul had become popular by now, and growing Korean nationalism in the 19th century, saw it’s usage even more. By 1894, Hangul was being used in official documents, elementary schools used it in 1895. Dongnip Sinmun, the first newspaper in Hangul was printed in 1896. However the literary elite still continued to use the traditional Chinese script.

Though Japanese became the official language during the occupation, Hangul was taught in most schools. They used a mixed Hanja-Hangul script, and it further evolved during the period. And when Korea became independent, it soon became the standard script.
